EVENT SYSTEM USAGE
==================

OVERVIEW
--------

This system allows publishers and subscribers to communicate without knowing
about each other.

Each event is:

    - Globally accessible.
    - Strongly typed.
    - Identified by its C++ class type.
    - Safe to subscribe to using an automatic Subscription handle.


STRICT EVENT RULE
-----------------

ONE EVENT CLASS MUST REPRESENT ONE UNIQUE EVENT CHANNEL.

Never use the same event class for two different meanings.

Incorrect:

    class DamagedEvent
        : public EventSystem::Event<DamagedEvent, int>
    {
    };

    // Both use the same global channel.
    class Player { DamagedEvent damaged; };
    class Enemy  { DamagedEvent damaged; };

Correct:

    class PlayerDamagedEvent
        : public EventSystem::Event<PlayerDamagedEvent, int>
    {
    };

    class EnemyDamagedEvent
        : public EventSystem::Event<EnemyDamagedEvent, int>
    {
    };


DEFINING EVENTS
---------------

The first template argument is always the event class itself.

The remaining arguments define the data carried by the event.

No arguments:

    class GameStartedEvent
        : public EventSystem::Event<GameStartedEvent>
    {
    };

One argument:

    class PlayerDamagedEvent
        : public EventSystem::Event<PlayerDamagedEvent, int>
    {
    };

Multiple arguments:

    class FileAddedEvent
        : public EventSystem::Event<
            FileAddedEvent,
            int,
            const std::string&
        >
    {
    };


PUBLISHING
----------

The publisher owns the event object and calls emit().

    class Player
    {
        public:
            void take_damage(int damage)
            {
                m_damaged_event.emit(damage);
            }

        private:
            PlayerDamagedEvent m_damaged_event;
    };

Arguments must match the event definition:

    m_damaged_event.emit(100);     // Correct
    m_damaged_event.emit();        // Compile error
    m_damaged_event.emit("100");   // Compile error


SUBSCRIBING
-----------

IMPORTANT:

ALWAYS STORE THE SUBSCRIPTION RETURNED BY subscribe().

If you do not store it, it is immediately destroyed and the callback is
immediately disconnected.


MEMBER FUNCTION
---------------

    class HealthBar
    {
        public:
            HealthBar()
            {
                m_subscription =
                    EventSystem::EventManager::get_instance()
                        .subscribe<PlayerDamagedEvent>(
                            this,
                            &HealthBar::on_player_damaged
                        );
            }

        private:
            EventSystem::Subscription m_subscription;

            void on_player_damaged(int damage)
            {
                // Handle the event.
            }
    };

When HealthBar is destroyed, m_subscription is destroyed and the callback is
automatically disconnected.


FREE OR STATIC FUNCTION
-----------------------

    void on_game_started()
    {
    }

    EventSystem::Subscription subscription =
        EventSystem::EventManager::get_instance()
            .subscribe<GameStartedEvent>(&on_game_started);


LAMBDA
------

    EventSystem::Subscription subscription =
        EventSystem::EventManager::get_instance()
            .subscribe<PlayerDamagedEvent>(
                [](int damage)
                {
                    // Handle the event.
                }
            );


MULTIPLE SUBSCRIPTIONS
----------------------

Store separate handles:

    EventSystem::Subscription m_damage_subscription;
    EventSystem::Subscription m_death_subscription;

Or store them in a vector:

    std::vector<EventSystem::Subscription> m_subscriptions;


MANUAL DISCONNECTION
--------------------

A subscription disconnects automatically when destroyed.

It can also be disconnected early:

    m_subscription.disconnect();

Check its state with:

    if (m_subscription.is_connected())
    {
    }


CONSTRUCTION ORDER
------------------

Publishers and subscribers may be created in any order.

The subscriber does not need to wait for the publisher.


IMPORTANT RULES
---------------

1. ONE EVENT CLASS = ONE UNIQUE GLOBAL EVENT CHANNEL.

2. The first Event template argument must be the event class itself.

3. emit() arguments must match the event definition.

4. Subscriber callback arguments must match the event definition.

5. ALWAYS STORE THE RETURNED Subscription.

6. Destroying the Subscription automatically disconnects the callback.

7. Publishers and subscribers must never directly depend on each other.


QUICK EXAMPLE
-------------

    class FileAddedEvent
        : public EventSystem::Event<
            FileAddedEvent,
            const std::string&
        >
    {
    };

Publisher:

    FileAddedEvent file_added_event;
    file_added_event.emit("image.png");

Subscriber:

    EventSystem::Subscription subscription =
        EventSystem::EventManager::get_instance()
            .subscribe<FileAddedEvent>(
                [](const std::string& file)
                {
                    std::cout << file << '\n';
                }
            );


FINAL REMINDER
--------------

ONE EVENT CLASS = ONE UNIQUE EVENT.

ALWAYS STORE THE RETURNED Subscription.

NO STORED Subscription = NO ACTIVE SUBSCRIPTION.
